sentences.text: Alexander Weissberg.

When he was arrested during the big purges by Stalin, he-- every Nobel Prize winner, Joliot-Curie, the daughter and his wife, and [INAUDIBLE], and including Albert Einstein, they wrote to Stalin about Alexander Weissberg that this must be a mistake, that he absolutely could not be involved in killing Stalin or Molotov or whatever.
But of course, it was no answer whatsoever.
However, he was so every day in this inquiry by-- by the KBG and so forth for two years or more.
And then-- but nothing ever happened.
And they keep him and exchange him with some other German and some other Jews who were there.
[INAUDIBLE] they give them into the German hands at the beginning of the war, because they were sure that they will kill them, the German will kill them all.
But Weissberg run away from the transport in Poland.
And he came into the Jewish-- into the Krakow ghetto.
And there, this is how he met me.
He was in-- in that Polish Underground.
Being a physicist, he was preparing all kind of, you know, these things to spraying, the bombs and so forth.
And then he wanted badly take me out of the  ghetto and, you know, in order to save me.
But I would never part with my family.
So I stay in ghetto.
And he left.
He just disappear.
When he disappear, he went and was-- first he was fighting later on.
Because this was also '42, in Jewish, well, uprising in Warsaw in the ghetto, and then in Polish uprising in ghetto in-- not in ghetto, but in Warsaw.
And later on, he was saved by a Polish aristocratic woman with whom he later lived in Paris.
Cybulski, Sophia Cybulski, C-Y-B-U-L-S-K-I.
C.
C, like Cecilia.
During the war, he was-- she fell in love with him.
The big house.
He was preparing all these bombs in the her apartment or in her other places.
And, you know, as soon he-- the war was over, he wrote these books which are in foreign languages-- [GERMAN] in German, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], and then and all-- in all languages.
And Churchill can see that he spoke the greatest for-- for all this iron-- well, this-- that-- that he make this word Iron Curtain.
And this was a great truth.
He was the very first one to open the [? lights ?] of communism and of Russia right after the war, in about '48 or so.
And it took so many years till the world finally find out what is it.
But anyway--
From him.
Yes, Alexander Weissberg [INAUDIBLE].

And then, well, in-- in that introduction to his books was written by this-- I forgot his name, who wrote, well, the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And everybody knew what was going on with the Gulags and everything.
And I think, a-ha, this was his.
And in his book, first I start looking if this is him.
And I really found many places very similar, his way of speaking and this, this, this.
So anyway, this was my great experience, very innocent.
And he always said, everybody would laugh completely knowing how innocent we spent time together.
But I was enjoying it tremendously.
And so did he.
And he-- half of my diary is full of him.
I was quoting him by word by word.
She was a wife of a Polish film actor, a very nice Polish woman, who fell deadly-- her husband was killed.
And she fell deadly in love with him.
And this is it.
But now everybody's dead.
Not only Alexander Weissberg, who die about 15 years ago, his wife, and even that woman who gave me --
--his photograph.
No, no.
We even did not know, at least I did not know, about the extermination camps.
There was a rumor that there is going to be a concentration camp for us in Plaszow.
But about Auschwitz, I did not know.
No, no.
I thought it will be like an arbeitslager.
Well, that this is not concentration camp.
But as a matter of fact--
Yeah, something like that.
Fliegerhorst, as a matter of fact, was not that bad.
Because this was Luftwaffe.
They were-- this was Air Force.
And they were not Gestapo.
And they were-- they were afraid of Gestapo as we were and of SS.
Yeah.
I don't know.
I think, because this is it.
Everybody was afraid of SS were.
Well, they--
The Air Force, they were soldiers.
They were flyers.
They were fighting.
They were on the front.
And they did not deal too much with politics.
As a matter of fact, very often the soldiers--
I don't know.
Because everybody in Germany was afraid.
No.
Everybody in Germany was afraid of anybody else.
Even the SS, when he was one so, and watching us, it was not so bad.
When there were only two, right away they start yelling [INAUDIBLE].
Because they were afraid of one another.
Not-- not with everyone.
Who was SOB was SOB all the time.
Well, but sometimes they were just simply-- they always, for example, told us, when somebody else come, move, move, move, move, move.
Don't-- don't just stand.
The diary--
--is a diary during this time.
I was always-- every girl was writing a diary.
And I was writing a diary since I knew how to write.
But they-- they were lost.
And then I was writing my diary all the time in-- in ghetto-- in the ghetto, and then even in Fliegerhorst, and even at the very beginning of Plaszow.
But then I sent all this, well, diaries to my Polish friend who saved them with photographs for me for after the war.
But in the concentration camp, later on in Auschwitz and all the others, you couldn't write a diary.
You had nothing.
So then--
Yes.
I got in-- in-- in Auschwitz, I got from my colleague in the work such a small, red notebook, so small that I could keep this in my hand.
And then I wrote my poems.
And then this is like as if my diary took a concise form of a poem.
All of them.
Well, no, but some of it was written afterwards.
Some of it has dates on it that is-- that is-- are-- that is quite current.
No, no.
This is different.
When I was transcribing my diary, then it came to me.
Like, for example, with that Jozef Hollander, who told me I will survive, and I will this and this.
At this point, I thought, this needs explanation.
And in order not to mix with the diary, I give the date when I was transcribing my diary.
And I wrote there.
Yeah.
In the ghetto.
Just before, pre-ghetto, ghetto, and beginning of Plaszow concentration camp.
It doesn't picks anything.
Then-- then my-- then I just-- it goes from one poem into another.
And I am fitting in between what-- what is there.
As a matter of fact, my diary is the only existing document who came out of the ghetto in Krakow, in Polish language, from the milieu of Polish Jewish intelligentsia.
There is no other written there with dates, with everything.
This books?
Yes.
This-- that diary was published by Polish Cultural Foundation in Toronto.
As a matter of fact, it was not published by the Jews.
I don't know why.
But when I sent-- transcribed this diary and I sent it to Yad Vashem, later on it was sent to a competition of Jews from Krakow in Tel Aviv.
And because this is the only document, well, not written in retrospect-- like was written about 40 years later in retrospect from the memories of the people who were there.
But my diary was really written there.
So this diary got an award on this competition there.
And that introduction to this diary was written by Gideon Hausner, who was the prosecutor of-- of Eichmann in Jerusalem.
And then it was supposed to be published there.
But they did not have money here.
There was also, well, I don't know.
But anyway, the Polish Cultural Foundation found out about this.
And they wanted to publish.
And they published it.
They published this, I think, 1987.
Yeah.
It's out of print.
But this books has-- well, maybe I-- I still have this main book of my diary with cover, which I make sketch, my drawings about it.
Yeah.
I hope.
And there was that motto that I choose for my diary, that if evil will try to get to me, lift your spirits so high that it could not reach you.
Zaleski.
A romantic.
I don't even remember.
I just know Zaleski.
Oh, yeah, Polish romantic poet.
Oh, yeah.
Oh, yeah.
In many ways, because there is always evil somewhere.
[LAUGHS] But when you just look at this and don't let this evil destroy you, maybe psychologically is more important than even physically.
Yeah.
Oh, yeah.
Oh, yeah.
Well, in that, as I mentioned, in the-- the Fliegerhorst--
Yeah.
Yeah.
Until [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] December 1943.
Well, it was--
Well, it was-- there were two.
There were different camps on the Fliegerhorst.
It was some was [NON-ENGLISH].
One was [NON-ENGLISH].
Arbeitslager.
Yeah, it was arbeitslager.
And we were-- and but they, later on when Plaszow was already there, we become [NON-ENGLISH] of Plaszow.
It means that we administratively belong to Plaszow.
And then Plaszow, they, one day people from Plaszow came.
And [INAUDIBLE] [? people ?] Amon Goeth, the commandant of Plaszow, came.
And they said that since we belong to them, we have to-- we have to be ruled by the same rules.
And therefore, when people in Plaszow were taken, all that jewelry and this and that, so should we give everything.
No.
We are still in Fliegerhorst.
Yeah.
Yeah, yeah.
We had barracks for us.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Of thee--
In Fliegerhorst.
We were working.
Of course, we were working and we-- with all menial works.
Sometimes when it just happened, partially in an office doing files or this or that.

But-- but actually, it was physical work, except that we were not-- well, there was know that they come and beat us up and this-- this, like in-- we heard terrible stories about that liquidation of ghetto in Krakow.
And-- and then when our-- my parents went to Plaszow--
Because we heard that they are-- we knew that when they-- the German are doing some kind of an action or transportation or deportation, there is always killing, stealing, beating, and so forth.
No.
Not that I know.
And I think it wasn't.
It was-- well, they were more or less-- at least they were not so hateful toward us.
We were lucky, all of us.
As a matter of fact, the largest percentage of people who survived from Krakow, they were this one who were, at this worst time, in Fliegerhorst, and not in-- in the ghetto in Krakow.
Well, the barracks, where-- we were the girls'-- womans' barracks.
We had rooms.
There were about 20 girls in one room.
They were double [INAUDIBLE] we have.
Well, it was like a YMCA summer camp, one could say--
--the barracks.
Well, we were sleeping on the double beds.
Well, we had--
Oh, well, straw mattresses.
We had our own from home, places, beddings, this and that.
Oh, yeah, of course, of course.
This was so that we were--
We were allowed--
--to take.
Yeah, sure, but depending how much space there is.
But then when-- when the-- get camp from Plaszow to Fliegerhorst, they painted our-- if you know, everything, dresses, like stripes, yellow stripes this--
--and this.
They came later on when we become part of-- part of Plaszow.
No, no, no.
Yeah.
Yeah.
This was usually we get up at 6 o'clock.
And about 7 o'clock we got our-- I don't know, some breakfast or something.
And before 8 o'clock--
Bread and margarine or bread and cheese or bread and-- and sometimes sausage and-- and these things.
No.
Well, we work-- It depends.
Usually some people, we came between-- from 8 o'clock between 12:00 and 1:00
00 and 2:00, we came for-- for soup back.
And then back to work and about 6 o'clock.
Oh, yeah.
Everything.
Well, street cleaning.
People were working in the washers.
People were working with tailor shop, with, well, like--
No.
We were not-- not doing these things.
We were just doing the menial work, scrubbing the floor, cleaning the windows, working on the [NON-ENGLISH].
Well, these kind of things.
Oh, yeah, sometimes.
I even did also this for quite a few months, doing files for exchange part for the airplanes in the magazine.
Not that I know.
The only difference was like this that we were being painted here--
--and here, everything--
--clothes, dresses.
Yeah, we stayed there.
And here, big number.
Yeah.
Yes,that's right.
That's right.
Everything was--
--work was the same.
No, no, no.
Well, as long we were under-- under-- with that Air Force, it was different.
It depends.
Maybe someone was really terrible, well, depending on the human character.
Right.
No. 1943, there was the end of this.
Because they said that-- in-- in-- about six months earlier, we were taken so that we belonged to Fliegerhorst.
Yeah.
And then they closed--
--the camp and took us to Plaszow, all of us.
Yeah.
But we were still there.
No, no, no.
But-- but then we were still at Fliegerhorst when Fliegerhorst was taken over administratively--
--by this.
But we were still in Fliegerhorst.
But in December, they closed the-- the camp and took us all to Plaszow.
Yeah.
I was there maybe for about a month, when I was taken to another camp, also of the Air Force.
Yeah.
Yeah, something like this.
I don't remember exactly.
Plaszow scared me to death.
I had absolutely no idea about.
I was when the moment I went there with the-- with these wires, with-- with that electric wires, with the terrible noises, with that terrible language being used there.
Noise.
Everybody-- well, you see, a sign of power it was that people were not talking, but yelling.
And everybody was yelling.
Yeah.
This was when you have power in camp, well, then you yell.
From everybody who wanted to be a powerful person.
From the [NON-ENGLISH], from the [NON-ENGLISH], from every block [NON-ENGLISH], from every Jewish policeman, from every couple, from everyone there, there was-- there was noise.
And there was the most dirty language possible.
By trucks.
Yeah.
I don't know.
Maybe there were four trucks for all of us--
--big trucks.
Ah, well, 3/4 of an hour, half an hour.
I have to tell--
